title: What does Trump really want from Venezuela | FT #shorts

transcript.text: What does Donald Trump really want from
Venezuela? First came the military
buildup in the Caribbean, the threats
and the strikes on small boats. Then US
security forces attacked, seizing
Nicholas Maduro and his wife in the dead
of night. They flew them to New York
where they're now in federal custody.
Trump says the US will run Venezuela,
but what's in it for the Americans?
Trump says he's acting to stop
Venezuela's alleged involvement in the
drugs trade. Federal prosecutors have
charged Maduro and others with drug
trafficking and narcoism related
offenses. The feds, among other things,
claimed the ousted leader doled out
diplomatic passports to drug traffickers
in a past role as foreign minister. But
analysts say Venezuela is a transit
route for Colombian cocaine, not a key
supplier itself. Trump has also spelt
out that this is about oil, at least in
large part. Venezuela holds the world's
largest oil reserves, but after years of
political unrest and economic chaos,
it's a small producer. Trump said US oil
companies would now rebuild the oil
infrastructure.
>> I'm also meeting with oil companies.
Let's go. You know, you know what that's
about. We got a lot of oil to drill,
which is going to bring down oil prices
even further.
>> Sharers and US oil companies have
surged, but it's still unclear how this
will work. This is about geopolitics,
too. After the attack, Trump made
belligerent remarks toward other
countries in the hemisphere. He claimed
Colombia's leftist president, Gustavo
Petro, had factories where he makes
cocaine, so he does have to watch his
ass, and the drug cartels were running
Mexico.
Trump also said the US needed Greenland
for national security reasons. That
prompted a push back from Denmark's
prime minister. After the US raid, Trump
also made reference to the Denroe
doctrine. That's his new spin on the
Monroe Doctrine, America's long-standing
policy of repelling foreign influence in
its hemisphere. Trump is now
aggressively reasserting it. Finally,
the military intervention could prove
popular with Trump's MAGA base. If it
goes smoothly, some of the hundreds of
thousands of Venezuelans living in the
US could return home. Trump may also be
in need of a boost. His popularity is
sagging because of high living costs and
his past social ties to the late
pedophile Jeffrey Epstein. It's still
too early to tell whether Trump's gambit
will pay off or hurt him, but it's safe
to say Venezuela is pushing other
stories out of the news.
